title: Reading the Premed curriculum map

objectives.statement: Trace a topic from its domain and module to its stable outcome ID and read the links that affect study order.
Distinguish prerequisite, cross-link, pathway, requirement, source-tag, and evidence-confidence information without turning a curriculum label into an admissions or truth claim.
Construct a defensible next-topic plan from a route filter, prerequisite state, and stated learning goal.

scenes.title: A map is a decision tool
scenes.contentHtml:
A map is a decision tool

You open a curriculum map because you need to decide what to learn next. The useful question is not “Where should I click?” but “Which identity, scope label, and dependency justify my next step?”

In this lesson you will practise three actions:

  1. locate a learning outcome inside a larger structure;
  2. separate study-order links from other relationships; and
  3. turn a route goal and current prerequisite state into a short plan.

No specialist science knowledge is assumed. You will work with text and tables, so drawing or visually tracing a network is optional.

Callout

This lesson teaches how to read the EmbeddedKnowledge curriculum map. It does not determine whether a school will admit you, replace a current institution-specific prerequisite audit, or certify that you have mastered any science outcome.

Deliberate exclusions

You will not learn the scientific content inside the mapped topics, calculate course completion, or build a personal admissions strategy. Those tasks require later lessons, demonstrated outcome evidence, or current information from the institution concerned.

scenes.title: Read before learning the legend
scenes.contentHtml:
Read before learning the legend

The following miniature map is invented for this diagnostic. Its labels do not describe the Premed course.

Domain: Sciences
  Module: Cells
    Topic A: membrane model
    Topic B: diffusion

Topic A --precedes?--> Topic B
Topic B --related?--- Topic C: osmosis

Without checking later scenes, write brief answers:

  1. Which label identifies the smallest assessable learning target?
  2. What extra information would you need before treating either line as a required study order?
  3. If Topic A is secure, what would justify choosing Topic B next?
  4. Which part of the map would you use to check whether this cluster belongs to your planned route?

Record confidence as low, medium, or high. Confidence is not a score; it will help you notice whether a later correction changes both your answer and your certainty.

Recovery route

If terms such as module, prerequisite, or route are unfamiliar, continue to The map's grammar. The diagnostic assumes no prior map-reading skill. If the nested text is difficult to parse, rewrite it as three lines—domain, module, topic—and answer from that list.

scenes.title: The map's grammar
scenes.contentHtml:
The map's grammar

A useful reading starts by separating five questions: What is this item? What contains it? What must normally come first? Which route includes it? What does its evidence label mean? Mixing those questions produces bad plans even when every displayed word is read correctly.

Three layers, one tracked target

Layer Instructional job What not to infer
Domain A broad curricular region A domain is not one mastery score.
Module A coherent cluster of related topics A module title is not an independently assessed outcome.
Topic An observable outcome tracked as an atomic mastery unit Its screen position is not its identity.

The stable topic ID carries identity when a title is improved or the display is rearranged. A plan should therefore name topic-... IDs, not “the third circle on the left.”

Two links with different jobs

A prerequisite link points from an earlier dependency to the outcome that depends on it. It asserts a normal learning order. A cross-link says that two topics illuminate one another; it does not choose which one must be studied first. Containment links answer the separate question of which domain or module owns a child node.

Read arrows in words. For A → B labelled prerequisite, say: “A should normally be secure before B.” For an A–B cross-link, say: “A and B are conceptually related; this edge alone specifies no order.” This verbal translation prevents visual direction or proximity from silently changing the relation.

Scope labels are not dependency links

requirement, core, pathway, sourceTags, and evidenceConfidence answer different questions:

  • requirement describes curricular placement. Its complete current legend is portable-core (common guaranteed minimum), medicine-bridge, pathway (required only for a named route or broad qualification), on-ramp, and enrichment (useful extension outside the guaranteed minimum);
  • core is the Boolean membership field used by the route rule for the common portable core; in the current graph it is derived from requirement: portable-core;
  • pathway is an array of route tags used for route filtering. Do not confuse a topic whose requirement value is pathway with the separate pathway array;
  • source tags identify evidence families used to justify placement, not membership of a route;
  • evidence confidence reports confidence in curricular placement, not the probability that a scientific claim is true and not the percentage of institutions requiring it.

For a named route that includes the common core, the effective topic set combines topics whose pathway array names that route with topics whose core field is true—equivalently, topics with requirement: portable-core in the current graph. A portable-core entry in the pathway array is not the field that supplies common-core membership for this rule. The portable-core route itself resolves to its own pathway-tagged set. After filtering, prerequisites still decide sequence: membership makes a topic relevant, not automatically ready.

Callout

A topic filter is not an instrumented route. A non-empty pathway-tag filter does not prove that the corresponding documented syllabus route is implemented. In curriculum version 1.0, PW-US, PW-BIO, and PW-QNT are instrumented. PW-UK and PW-ES are documented but not instrumented: the uk-direct-entry and spain-bach-pau tags filter relevant content, but they are not implementations of PW-UK or PW-ES and cannot establish pathway completion. Confirm route status before using a filtered topic set as a study route.

A four-pass planning model

  1. Identity: locate the target by stable topic ID and confirm its outcome statement.
  2. Scope: confirm that the documented route is currently instrumented, then apply its route rule and inspect requirement and pathway labels.
  3. Order: follow prerequisite edges backward until you reach outcomes already secure or outcomes with no unmet dependencies.
  4. Evidence and boundary: read placement evidence honestly, then verify any external admissions requirement with its current primary source.

The model assumes that the published graph is current enough for curriculum planning and that your mastery record is stored separately. It does not infer mastery from time, confidence, or having opened a lesson.

Source Note
claims claim-map-layers, claim-map-link-types, claim-map-route-resolution, claim-map-label-scope, claim-map-stable-identitysources source-premed-knowledge-graph

The project knowledge-graph specification defines these node layers, link meanings, route rule, label boundaries, and identity conventions. The claims ledger records their current-version scope.

scenes.title: Build a route to a study plan
scenes.contentHtml:
Build a route to a study plan

Task

A learner wants to reach topic-orientation-study-plan. Build the shortest prerequisite-respecting route shown by the current map, and explain what the route does and does not establish.

Givens and representation

The map records this chain:

Outcome ID Direct prerequisite
topic-orientation-map-and-pathways none
topic-orientation-diagnostic-baseline topic-orientation-map-and-pathways
topic-orientation-mastery-and-retrieval topic-orientation-diagnostic-baseline
topic-orientation-metacognition-and-error-log topic-orientation-mastery-and-retrieval
topic-orientation-study-plan topic-orientation-metacognition-and-error-log

The unknown is the justified study order. The relevant relation is prerequisite, because the task asks about sequence; containment and cross-links cannot answer that question.

Worked Example
id worked-example-orientation-route
  1. Select the model. Use directed prerequisite links, not screen position or module order.
  2. Plan. Start at the target, follow each prerequisite backward, stop at a node without a prerequisite, then reverse the collected list.
  3. Execute. The resulting order is map-and-pathways → diagnostic-baseline → mastery-and-retrieval → metacognition-and-error-log → study-plan.
  4. Check direction. In every adjacent pair, the source is the listed prerequisite of the next outcome. The first topic has no graph prerequisite, so the backward trace is complete.
  5. Interpret. If none of these outcomes is yet secure, begin with map-and-pathways. If the first three are already demonstrated, begin with metacognition-and-error-log; do not replay secure work solely because it appears earlier.
  6. State the boundary. The chain gives a curriculum dependency, not proof of mastery, a required calendar, or an admissions rule.

The table and the arrow sentence carry the same relation. The table is convenient for checking one dependency at a time; the arrow chain makes the whole order easier to summarize. Neither representation is the learner's actual progress record.

Self-explanation prompt: Which decision mattered most—choosing a target, recognizing the link type, or reversing the backward trace? Explain why choosing the wrong link type would invalidate the rest of the solution.

Source Note
claims claim-map-layers, claim-map-link-types, claim-map-label-scope, claim-map-stable-identitysources source-premed-knowledge-graph

The worked example applies the current graph's stable topic records and directed prerequisite semantics. It makes no claim that completing or viewing a node demonstrates mastery.

scenes.title: A connection is not always an order
scenes.contentHtml:
A connection is not always an order

Commit to a prediction

Suppose a map contains A → B as a prerequisite and a cross-link between B and C. Does the map require the order A, then B, then C? Answer before reading on.

Misconception
id misconception-every-edge-is-order

An understandable but inadequate rule is: “Every visible connection tells me what comes next.” It works for the A-to-B prerequisite, then fails at the B-to-C cross-link. The cross-link records conceptual relevance without imposing sequence, so it cannot place C after B.

A more adequate rule first reads the edge type. Use prerequisite edges for ordering, containment for hierarchy, and cross-links for integration or later comparison. On the stated evidence, only A before B is required; C's eligibility depends on C's own prerequisites.

Recheck in a changed case

Topic D and Topic E share a module. E has no declared prerequisite, while D has a cross-link to E. Which topic must come first?

The map fragment imposes no order between them. Choose by the learner's goal, route, and other prerequisite records. Sharing a container or a cross-link is not a hidden dependency.

The same discipline applies to labels. A high placement-confidence label does not mean “study immediately,” and a route tag does not mean “prerequisites satisfied.”

Source Note
claims claim-map-link-types, claim-map-label-scopesources source-premed-knowledge-graph

The repair contrasts the graph specification's ordering and non-ordering relations and keeps scope labels separate from readiness.

scenes.title: Fade the map-reading support
scenes.contentHtml:
Fade the map-reading support

These fictional fragments isolate the map-reading operation. Attempt each prompt before reading its feedback.

Completion problem

Complete the hierarchy: domain → ______ → topic. Then state which layer should hold an independently tracked observable outcome.

Feedback after an attempt: insert module; the topic is the atomic outcome. If you chose domain, you grouped too broadly. Next, practise locating the topic's stable ID rather than relying on its title.

Supported route filter

Route R is declared to be instrumented and to include the common core. Two topics have pathway: ["R"]; three other topics have core: true because their requirement is portable-core; and one topic has only pathway: ["S"] with core: false. Which sets enter Route R before prerequisite ordering?

Hint: route relevance and learning readiness are separate decisions.

Feedback after an attempt: because R is explicitly instrumented, combine the R-tagged topics with the three core: true topics. Do not include the S-only, core: false topic. Common-core membership comes from core/requirement: portable-core, not from a portable-core route tag. Without the instrumentation premise, the same union would be only a content filter. Your next step is still to inspect prerequisites; a relevant topic may remain unavailable.

Independent edge selection

A fictional learner has demonstrated Topic K. Topic L lists K as a prerequisite. Topic M has only a cross-link to K. Select the next topic that the fragment justifies and name the relation that warrants your choice.

Feedback after an attempt: L is justified because its stated prerequisite is met. The cross-link makes M worth inspecting but cannot establish readiness. If you selected M, return to the edge-type translation in The map's grammar.

Mixed label discrimination

A topic is marked evidenceConfidence: medium, carries a sourceTags entry from an exam outline, and is tagged for a pathway. Write one warranted conclusion and two conclusions that the labels do not warrant.

Feedback after an attempt: a warranted conclusion concerns the topic's placement in the named route and the recorded evidence family. Do not infer that the scientific content is only “medium true,” that every institution requires it, or that the learner has met its prerequisites.

For an independent scored attempt, complete item-map-order, item-cross-link-order, and item-label-boundary in assessment.json. Retry with the equivalent fragments described in the recovery route rather than memorizing option positions.

Source Note
claims claim-map-layers, claim-map-link-types, claim-map-route-resolution, claim-map-label-scope, claim-map-stable-identitysources source-premed-knowledge-graph

The practice varies hierarchy, route filtering, edge selection, and label interpretation while applying the same graph contract.

scenes.title: Reconstruct the legend
scenes.contentHtml:
Reconstruct the legend

Close the concept and worked-example scenes. Write from memory before checking the compact model below.

Check
id retrieval-map-legend
  1. Name the three node layers from broadest to most atomic.
  2. Say a prerequisite arrow in a complete sentence.
  3. Contrast a prerequisite with a cross-link.
  4. State what must be confirmed before treating a route-tag filter as a study route; then explain how an instrumented route that includes the common core combines its route-tagged topics with the common spine and name the field that identifies common-core membership.
  5. Explain what evidenceConfidence does not measure.

Record confidence before checking. Then compare your response with this model: domain → module → topic; a prerequisite normally precedes its dependent; a cross-link adds conceptual relation but no order; first confirm that the documented route is currently instrumented, because a tag filter alone does not establish route implementation; an instrumented route that includes the common core combines its pathway-tagged topics with topics whose core field is true (requirement: portable-core in the current graph); evidence confidence concerns curricular placement, not factual truth or universal institutional demand.

If one distinction was missing, retrieve only that distinction again after a short unrelated task. If two or more were missing, reconstruct the four-pass model before attempting the transfer.

Source Note
claims claim-map-layers, claim-map-link-types, claim-map-route-resolution, claim-map-label-scopesources source-premed-knowledge-graph

The retrieval prompts reconstruct the same bounded node, link, route, and label semantics defined by the current specification.

scenes.title: Adapt the map to a real decision
scenes.contentHtml:
Adapt the map to a real decision

A fictional learner, Noor, plans to apply to Harbor Medical School next year. Harbor's current website lists an institution-specific prerequisite that Noor cannot find as a Premed topic title. Noor also wants the currently instrumented Premed PW-US route.

This is not a request to guess Harbor's rule. It is a route-planning problem with an external-boundary check.

Your task

Write a five-step plan that:

  1. confirms the documented route is instrumented in the current curriculum version, then resolves its effective topic set by combining its pathway-tagged topics with core: true topics when that route includes the common core;
  2. locates candidate content by outcome meaning and stable ID rather than title matching alone;
  3. traces unmet prerequisite links;
  4. treats cross-links and evidence labels according to their actual jobs; and
  5. records a separate check of Harbor's current primary-source requirement.

Then identify the first topic Noor should study only if the supplied mastery record shows its prerequisites secure. The correct response is conditional: the curriculum map can organize a route, but it cannot supply missing external policy or infer mastery.

This transfer differs from the worked example. The target is not already named by an exact graph ID, the route filter and dependency order must be coordinated, and one decision belongs outside the graph. Complete item-route-plan in assessment.json for the scored version.

Source Note
claims claim-map-layers, claim-map-link-types, claim-map-route-resolution, claim-map-label-scope, claim-map-stable-identitysources source-premed-knowledge-graph

The transfer applies the graph contract while preserving two explicit boundaries: a pathway tag is not proof that a documented route is instrumented, and pathway labels are curriculum filters rather than institution-specific admissions promises.

scenes.title: Your map-reading card
scenes.contentHtml:
Your map-reading card

Before choosing a next topic, write five lines:

  1. Identity: target stable topic ID and observable outcome.
  2. Scope: current route-instrumentation status, then requirement and effective route membership.
  3. Order: unmet prerequisite chain, read from prerequisite to dependent.
  4. Evidence: what the placement labels support and what they do not.
  5. Action: the earliest relevant outcome whose prerequisites are secure, plus the evidence used to judge “secure.”

Use cross-links after or alongside this plan to connect ideas; do not silently turn them into locks. Treat a non-empty route-tag set as a content filter until the current curriculum version confirms that the documented route is instrumented. Keep institution-specific requirements in a dated external audit rather than inferring them from graph tags.

Source Note
claims claim-map-layers, claim-map-link-types, claim-map-route-resolution, claim-map-label-scope, claim-map-stable-identitysources source-premed-knowledge-graph

This card compresses the sourced graph-reading model without changing the meanings or the admissions boundary recorded in the claims ledger.

Accessibility and alternatives

Every relationship in this lesson is written as a sentence or table row; no answer depends on color, coordinates, visual tracing, audio, hover, dragging, or time pressure. A learner may respond with an ordered list, a table, or spoken text transcribed by an accessible tool. The operation remains the same: identify the node, name the link type, justify scope, and select a next action.

Recovery route

  • If hierarchy is unstable, rebuild three lines—domain, module, topic—and retry item-map-order with new labels.
  • If order is unstable, translate each edge into a sentence before retrying item-cross-link-order with a different fragment.
  • If scope labels are overinterpreted, write “placement, not truth or admission” beside evidenceConfidence, then retry item-label-boundary in your own words.
  • If the full plan fails, return to the worked example, mark the first unsupported inference, and retry item-route-plan with one fewer hint. Unlimited untimed attempts are permitted.

Delayed retrieval

In the next lesson on diagnostic baselines, retrieve the difference between curriculum identity and learner evidence before interpreting a placement result. Reconstruct this five-line card again after at least two days and when a later lesson first asks you to open an unfamiliar prerequisite chain.

aiAssistance.disclosure: An OpenAI Codex authoring run designed, researched, drafted, and self-audited this local candidate under the accountable principal VSBDev. This disclosure is author provenance, not independent review or evidence of learner effectiveness.

assessment.title: Curriculum map navigation and route planning

items.prompt: Order the node layers from the broadest curricular grouping to the independently tracked observable outcome.

answer.summary: A domain contains modules, and a module contains atomic topic outcomes.
answer.reasoning: The layers answer different identity questions. Domain is the broad region, module is a coherent cluster, and topic is the observable outcome tracked independently.
commonErrors: Treating a module as the smallest tracked outcome
Ordering by visual size or screen position instead of node kind

items.prompt: Topic A is a prerequisite of Topic B. Topic B has a cross-link to Topic C. Which order is justified by those two links alone?

options.text: A must normally precede B; no B–C order is established.
A must precede B, and B must precede C.
C must precede B, and B must precede A.

answer.summary: Only the prerequisite link establishes order.
answer.reasoning: A prerequisite edge directs study from A to B. A cross-link records conceptual connection without direction, so C's place depends on its own prerequisite record and the learner's goal.
commonErrors: Treating every visible edge as a prerequisite
Reading visual proximity as an ordering rule

items.prompt: A topic has evidenceConfidence set to medium and carries a pathway tag. State what each label supports and name two claims the labels do not justify.

answer.correct: Evidence confidence qualifies confidence in curricular placement, and the pathway tag supports route filtering. Neither establishes that the scientific content is only partly true, that every institution requires the topic, that the learner has mastered it, or that its prerequisites are satisfied.
answer.summary: Placement and route labels have bounded curricular meanings.
answer.reasoning: Label interpretation must preserve the question each field answers. Truth, individual mastery, readiness, and current institution-specific requirements require different evidence.
acceptableResponses: Any response that accurately identifies placement confidence and route membership and rejects at least two unwarranted inferences about truth, mastery, readiness, or universal admissions requirements.
commonErrors: Reading medium as a probability that the science is true
Treating a pathway tag as proof of a universal admissions requirement
Treating route relevance as prerequisite completion

items.prompt: Construct a five-step plan for a learner who has named a route and an institution-specific goal but has not found an exact title match in the graph. Coordinate current route-instrumentation status, stable IDs, effective route membership, prerequisite order, cross-links, placement evidence, mastery evidence, and a current external requirement check.
items.stimulus: The learner's record identifies some secure outcomes, but no result may be inferred merely from lesson completion or confidence. The institution's current primary-source requirement is not included in the graph excerpt.

answer.correct: First confirm that the documented route is instrumented in the current curriculum version; a non-empty pathway-tag filter alone is not proof. If the instrumented route includes the common core, resolve it as topics whose pathway array names the route plus topics whose core field is true (derived from requirement: portable-core in the current graph); search outcome meanings and confirm candidate stable IDs; trace candidate prerequisites backward and compare them with demonstrated mastery; use cross-links only to surface related learning and read placement labels within scope; choose the earliest relevant candidate whose prerequisites are secure while separately verifying the institution's current requirement from its primary source.
answer.summary: A defensible plan separates curriculum identity, route scope, dependency order, learner evidence, and external policy.
answer.reasoning: No single map field answers all five questions. Stable IDs protect identity, route labels filter relevance, prerequisites order learning, a separate mastery record supports readiness, and only the institution's current source can establish its rule.
acceptableResponses: Equivalent five-step plans are acceptable when they preserve all boundaries and make the next-topic choice conditional on demonstrated prerequisites.
commonErrors: Filtering on source tags instead of the route rule
Using a cross-link as a lock
Inferring mastery from completion or confidence
Treating the map as a current admissions authority

criteria.description: Places domain, module, and topic in the correct containment order.
Uses the prerequisite edge to order A before B.
Declines to infer an order between B and C from a cross-link.
States the placement-confidence and route-filter meanings accurately.
Rejects at least two unwarranted truth, admissions, mastery, or readiness inferences.

Checks that the documented route is currently instrumented, then resolves a common-core route using its pathway-tagged topics plus topics with core: true rather than treating a portable-core pathway tag as the common-core test.
Uses stable outcome identity and traces prerequisite order correctly.
Separates cross-links, placement evidence, mastery evidence, and external policy.
Makes the next action conditional on demonstrated prerequisite security and names a recovery or verification step.

sources.title: Premed Knowledge Graph
sources.locator: Sections 2, 3, 5.1, and 6; canonical dataset site/data/premed-graph.json keys schema.routeResolutionRule, schema.requirementLegend, and metrics.routeTopicCounts
sources.license: CC BY 4.0 under the repository CONTENT-LICENSE.md for course/ and site/data/

uses.rightsBasisEvidence: The lesson independently organizes and expresses the graph's data contract, node and link semantics, route rule, and scope boundaries; it copies no source examples, tables, prose sequence, or media.

agentAccess.notes: The agent used the operator-provided local repository checkout. The repository's content license expressly covers course/ and site/data/, and no source-specific restriction on agent processing was found in the applicable project policies.

claims.statement: The Premed graph uses domains for broad curricular regions, modules for coherent clusters, and topics for independently tracked observable outcomes.
claims.scope: EmbeddedKnowledge Premed knowledge-graph schema version 1.0.0 and curriculum version 1.0 candidate.
claims.uncertainty: The graph structure may change in a future version; stable published identity and migration rules should then govern interpretation.

claims.statement: A Premed prerequisite edge directs normal learning order from dependency to dependent, whereas a cross-link records a conceptual relation without imposing order.
claims.scope: The link semantics declared by the current Premed graph data contract.

claims.statement: A named Premed route that includes the common core resolves to the union of topics whose pathway array names that route and topics whose core field is true, which in the current graph is derived from requirement: portable-core; source tags and portable-core pathway tags do not replace that core-membership test.
claims.scope: Current graph route-resolution rule in schema.routeResolutionRule and its implementation: route pathway membership OR core membership when includesPortableCore is true; the portable-core route resolves to its own pathway-tagged topic set. Resolution is a content-filter operation and does not by itself establish that a documented syllabus route is instrumented.
claims.uncertainty: In curriculum version 1.0, PW-US, PW-BIO, and PW-QNT are instrumented. PW-UK and PW-ES are documented but not instrumented; uk-direct-entry and spain-bach-pau are content-filter tags rather than implementations of those routes. Instrumentation and membership may change in a later version.

claims.statement: Requirement, pathway, source-tag, and evidence-confidence labels describe curricular scope and placement evidence, not scientific truth probability, individual mastery, prerequisite completion, or universal admissions requirements.
claims.scope: Interpretation boundary for fields in the current Premed knowledge graph.
claims.uncertainty: Institution-specific admission requirements are external, time-sensitive sources and are not established by these fields.

claims.statement: Premed mastery records should use stable topic IDs rather than array position, title, or visual coordinates.
claims.scope: Identity and versioning rules for the current Premed graph.
claims.uncertainty: Retired or split IDs require migration records in later graph versions.

terms.definition: A broad curricular region that contains graph modules and does not itself represent one atomic mastery outcome.
A coherent graph-scale cluster of related topics inside a domain.
The atomic graph unit whose outcome can be tracked independently in a mastery record.
An outcome that should normally be secure before a dependent outcome is attempted.
A conceptual relationship between topics that does not by itself impose a study order.

In the pathway array, a route tag used with the published resolution rule to select a relevant topic set. Separately, pathway is also a permitted value of the requirement field; that value describes curricular placement and is not the route-tag array.
The Boolean field used by route resolution for common portable-core membership; in the current graph it is derived from requirement: portable-core.
The graph's confidence in a topic's curricular placement, not a probability that its scientific content is true.
A key identifying an evidence family used to support curricular placement; it is not a route-membership rule.

attributionHtml:
Attribution

PREM-LPP-001, version 0.1.4, was prepared for EmbeddedKnowledge under the accountable GitHub principal VSBDev and is offered under CC BY 4.0. The lesson remains a local draft and is not adjudicated, published, or evidence of course coverage. Claim review states and lesson source confidence are pending for this exact candidate. Historical review artifacts remain in the pack for traceability but do not count toward the fresh role quorum required before adjudication.

The prose, fictional diagnostic fragments, worked reasoning, practice, transfer case, assessment items, answer logic, and glossary were newly created for this lesson. No third-party media, quotations, adapted expression, or dataset extraction is included.

Source use and rights

The EmbeddedKnowledge Premed Knowledge Graph specification and its canonical local dataset were used as factual references for the project's current node layers, link semantics, route-resolution rule, label boundaries, and stable-identity conventions. The lesson uses a different instructional sequence and independently written examples and wording. The source is covered by the repository's CC BY 4.0 content license, but this pack relies only on facts-only original expression. The complete dated use and agent-access record appears in references.json.

Agent disclosure

Material authoring assistance came from Codex, provider OpenAI, model GPT-5, running through Codex CLI 0.144.6, run ID codex-author-019f7c89-e690-7180-bba6-a1602a1de10a.

The current revision digest is sha256:ac9d1635c6afe0ca7d7ab8e1b556b837177ab72709716cf96f4e61a007ba69b2. It extends the prior revision digest with the exact version-0.1.3 academic review artifact that identified the present route-instrumentation boundary and the unobservable hierarchy-rubric criterion addressed in version 0.1.4. The prior revision digest was sha256:ae71d74d89474e02fb0d3d6739c30a79aba9afba19f65585e8caaa220e1eb851; it extended the governance-promotion digest with the earlier final academic review artifact. The governance-promotion digest was sha256:f88af583438efad9ca71d34da67c4e2f83de8f3e5743b8583394071d50cf87fc; it is the SHA-256 of the prior author-revision digest line followed, in filename order, by the exact cycle-2 academic, learning-design, and accessibility/rights review artifact bytes used to promote retained claim states and source confidence. The earlier author-revision digest was sha256:0d2e6113c37a593b2bd7f3a0d3070fb95f4adf2beca8e2a4e0fd6d9ae1270ec3; the original digest was sha256:2b049821cfb96565b5ce4263b1b3400e57c6feddd409547b0d84346e7762a22f. These digests exclude hidden provider instructions and do not claim to identify a model snapshot that the runtime did not expose.

Agent provenance is disclosed and attested by the accountable principal; it is not independently verified and does not count as a review.
